monastery or courts were one resided, there were some generalities. The arithmetica consisted
of performing calculations on a counting board and with help of roman numerals, the theory of
ratios (as an introduction to musica), biblical numerology and the computus rules. Many Christian
thinkers tended to agree upon the date of June 12, 5012 BC at noon, as the time for Creation. In a
time that the Bible was considered the most important text, one can imagine that these kind of
calculations were of the utmost importance. Musica consisted of the theory of chords in connection
to ratios of string length, and the ways to indicate notes. The art of geometria was mostly
restricted to knowledge of the names of various geometrical objects. Triangles, cones, spheres
and the regular solids were interesting for their connection to the world order. The equilateral
triangle, for example, was viewed in connection to the Holy Trinity. Mostly the objects themselves
were considered fascinating: proofs were not considered important, although use of the
Pythagorean theorem was known. Finally, astronomia consisted of knowledge about movement
of the stars and planets on the basis of the Ptolemaic system. The connection with astrology was
not always popular, since it seemed to deny the possibility of a free choice of will. At courts,
however, trust in or need for predictions was in cases considered more important than religious
piety. So in practice, the knowledge of astrology was still valued and this implied interest in geometria
as well.

I'm planning to make a salad for dinner tonight and I want to use some fresh herbs. Can you give me some advice on how to keep my herbs fresh for a longer period? By the way, I started watering my herb garden every morning today, and I'm excited to see them grow.

I've been tracking my progress on a gardening app, and I noticed that my cherry tomato plants have been growing rapidly since I started fertilizing them every week with a balanced fertilizer. I'm thinking of experimenting with homemade fertilizers, but I'm not sure which one to try first. Do you think the Epsom salt fertilizer or the banana peel fertilizer would be a good starting point?

I think I'll try the banana peel fertilizer first. I'll bury a few banana peels around the base of my cherry tomato plants and see how they respond. I'll also keep an eye on their growth and adjust my fertilization schedule as needed. Thanks for the advice! By the way, do you have any tips on how to create a DIY drip irrigation system? I'm interested in saving water and reducing evaporation.

I'll definitely give it a try! I've been wanting to reduce my water usage and this seems like a great way to do it. I'll start planning my DIY drip irrigation system and see how it goes. Thanks for the tips and advice!
I'm looking for some new recipe ideas that feature fresh herbs. I just harvested my first batch of fresh herbs from the herb garden kit today and I'm excited to use them in different dishes.
I'm particularly interested in the Thai Basil Chicken Stir-Fry and the Pesto Pasta. Can you give me some tips on how to store my fresh herbs to keep them fresh for a longer period?

I'd like to know more about composting. I started creating a compost bin in my backyard using a wooden pallet and started adding vegetable scraps, eggshells, and coffee grounds to it. How often should I turn the compost pile and what are some other materials I can add to it?

I'm curious about the different types of irrigation systems. I recently implemented a drip irrigation system in my backyard, and I'm interested in learning more about other options. Can you tell me about the different types of irrigation systems and their advantages?

My Instagram handle is @username123, I have around 200 posts, and I'd like to analyze my posts from the last 3 months. I mostly post personal stuff, like photos of my daily life and hobbies. By the way, speaking of photos, I posted a pic of my cat Luna a while back and it got a surprising 57 likes, which is still my highest engagement in a while.

Read the chapter 15 { 
CHAPTER FIFTEEN
 RULE #6
 Crawl Before You Walk, Walk Before You Run
 From Jeremy
As you know by now, the essence of the protocol is lifelong behavioral change. That means changing the way you carry yourself and move (the neutral spine and all that). But it also means adopting and staying with our carefully designed (and progressive) exercise regimen. It is designed for back pain and it is essential for a permanent cure. And you have to do it correctly. There is an awful lot of text and a bunch of pictures about exercise in this book. But it’s not because you have to do so many exercises; there really aren’t that many. It’s because we want you to do them right. Remember, you are far more likely to do these exercises with the compensatory patterns you have been doing for years unless you read closely and concentrate on doing them the right way. Doing them the wrong way will just make you better at doing bad things. 
One great key to doing them right is to go slow in the learning process. Whether you’re the type or not, you have to take it step-by-step. This is a healing process, and you cannot rush a healing process; it has its own rhythm and you have to follow it. Roll out these exercises slowly over time, progressing from one level to the next only when you “get” the level you are on and are ready to move up. 
Remember, it is these daily exercises that—more than anything else—are going to give most of you a significant and permanent reduction in pain and tightness. Other steps get you past the immediate pain. These steps make the  …[truncated]
